examples.explanation:
Start: max_sum([3,2,5,10,7], 0)
At i=0: include=3+max_sum(arr,2)=3+?; exclude=max_sum(arr,1)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,2): i=2: include=5+max_sum(arr,4)=5+?; exclude=max_sum(arr,3)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,4): i=4: include=7+max_sum(arr,6)=7+0=7; exclude=max_sum(arr,5)=0; max=7
Compute max_sum(arr,3): i=3: include=10+max_sum(arr,5)=10+0=10; exclude=max_sum(arr,4)=7; max=10
Back to i=2: include=5+7=12; exclude=10; max=12
Compute max_sum(arr,1): i=1: include=2+max_sum(arr,3)=2+10=12; exclude=max_sum(arr,2)=12; max=12
Back to i=0: include=3+12=15; exclude=12; max=15
Final output: 15

Start: max_sum([5,1,2,6], 0)
At i=0: include=5+max_sum(arr,2)=5+?; exclude=max_sum(arr,1)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,2): i=2: include=2+max_sum(arr,4)=2+0=2; exclude=max_sum(arr,3)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,3): i=3: include=6+max_sum(arr,5)=6+0=6; exclude=max_sum(arr,4)=0; max=6
Back to i=2: include=2; exclude=6; max=6
Compute max_sum(arr,1): i=1: include=1+max_sum(arr,3)=1+6=7; exclude=max_sum(arr,2)=6; max=7
Back to i=0: include=5+6=11; exclude=7; max=11
Final output: 11

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsets of elements, skipping adjacent ones, and compute the maximum sum. Use it for understanding the problem but not for practical solutions due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsets while avoiding adjacent elements to find maximum sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the maximum sum of non-adjacent elements problem. The intuition is to systematically explore every possible subset of elements from the input array while respecting the constraint that no two adjacent elements can be selected. We achieve this through recursion where at each index, we have two choices: either include the current element and skip the next one (to maintain non-adjacency), or exclude the current element and move to the next. This creates a binary decision tree with 2^n leaf nodes, representing all possible valid subsets. The algorithm starts from index 0 and recursively computes the maximum sum by considering both inclusion and exclusion cases, eventually returning the maximum value found across all paths. The time complexity is O(2^n) because we explore all subsets, and space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion stack depth. This approach serves as a foundational understanding of the problem but is impractical for large inputs due to exponential growth. Connection to L1: This full solution directly implements the recursive helper function from L1 within the main problem context. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases, incorrectly handling array bounds, and not considering both inclusion/exclusion scenarios properly. Real code pattern: def max_sum(arr, i): if i >= len(arr): return 0; include = arr[i] + max_sum(arr, i+2); exclude = max_sum(arr, i+1); return max(include, exclude).

examples.trace:
Start: max_sum([3,2,5,10,7], 0)
At i=0: include=3+max_sum(arr,2)=3+?; exclude=max_sum(arr,1)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,2): i=2: include=5+max_sum(arr,4)=5+?; exclude=max_sum(arr,3)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,4): i=4: include=7+max_sum(arr,6)=7+0=7; exclude=max_sum(arr,5)=0; max=7
Compute max_sum(arr,3): i=3: include=10+max_sum(arr,5)=10+0=10; exclude=max_sum(arr,4)=7; max=10
Back to i=2: include=5+7=12; exclude=10; max=12
Compute max_sum(arr,1): i=1: include=2+max_sum(arr,3)=2+10=12; exclude=max_sum(arr,2)=12; max=12
Back to i=0: include=3+12=15; exclude=12; max=15
Final output: 15

Start: max_sum([5,1,2,6], 0)
At i=0: include=5+max_sum(arr,2)=5+?; exclude=max_sum(arr,1)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,2): i=2: include=2+max_sum(arr,4)=2+0=2; exclude=max_sum(arr,3)=?
Compute max_sum(arr,3): i=3: include=6+max_sum(arr,5)=6+0=6; exclude=max_sum(arr,4)=0; max=6
Back to i=2: include=2; exclude=6; max=6
Compute max_sum(arr,1): i=1: include=1+max_sum(arr,3)=1+6=7; exclude=max_sum(arr,2)=6; max=7
Back to i=0: include=5+6=11; exclude=7; max=11
Final output: 11

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for Maximum Sum at Index i
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes maximum sum starting from a given index while avoiding adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive helper function that forms the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that at any index i in the array, the maximum sum of non-adjacent elements starting from i can be computed by considering two mutually exclusive cases: either we include the element at index i and then recursively compute the maximum sum starting from i+2 (skipping the adjacent element at i+1), or we exclude the element at i and recursively compute starting from i+1. By taking the maximum of these two cases, we ensure we explore all valid subsets. The base case occurs when i is beyond the array bounds, returning 0. This recursive formulation naturally captures the constraint of non-adjacency through the i+2 jump when including an element. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^n) in the worst case as it explores all subsets, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main solution in L0 with i=0 to solve the complete problem. Common mistakes include incorrect base case conditions (using i == len(arr) instead of i >= len(arr) to handle i+2 jumps), and not properly handling the recursive calls. Real code pattern: def helper(arr, i): if i >= len(arr): return 0; include = arr[i] + helper(arr, i+2); exclude = helper(arr, i+1); return max(include, exclude).

examples.trace:
helper([3,2,5], 0)
i=0: include=3+helper(arr,2)=3+?; exclude=helper(arr,1)=?
helper(arr,2): i=2: include=5+helper(arr,4)=5+0=5; exclude=helper(arr,3)=0; max=5
helper(arr,1): i=1: include=2+helper(arr,3)=2+0=2; exclude=helper(arr,2)=5; max=5
Back to i=0: include=3+5=8; exclude=5; max=8
Output: 8

helper([4,1,3], 1)
i=1: include=1+helper(arr,3)=1+0=1; exclude=helper(arr,2)=?
helper(arr,2): i=2: include=3+helper(arr,4)=3+0=3; exclude=helper(arr,3)=0; max=3
Back to i=1: include=1; exclude=3; max=3
Output: 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision Tree for Inclusion/Exclusion Choices
ladders.desc: Understanding the binary decision process of including or excluding each element while maintaining non-adjacency.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logical structure of the brute force approach: the binary decision tree that emerges from choosing to include or exclude each element while respecting the non-adjacency constraint. The intuition is that at each position in the array, we face a fundamental choice that branches our exploration into two paths. If we include the current element, we must skip the next element (jump to i+2) to avoid adjacency. If we exclude the current element, we can consider the next element (move to i+1). This creates a tree where each node represents a decision point, and leaves represent complete valid subsets. The maximum sum is found by traversing all paths and taking the maximum value. This decision process is recursive in nature, with each recursive call representing a subtree. Time complexity analysis shows 2^n total decisions in worst case, and space is O(n) for the recursion depth. Connection to L1: This decision logic is implemented in the recursive helper function from L1 through the include/exclude cases. Common mistakes include not understanding why i+2 is used for inclusion, or confusing the order of decisions. Real code pattern: The logic is embodied in: if include_current: sum1 = current_value + recurse(i+2); if exclude_current: sum2 = recurse(i+1); return max(sum1, sum2).

examples.trace:
Start at index 0:
Option 1: Include 2 → skip index 1 → go to index 2
  At index 2: Include 3 → sum=2+3=5; Exclude 3 → sum=2+0=2
Option 2: Exclude 2 → go to index 1
  At index 1: Include 4 → skip index 2 → sum=0+4=4; Exclude 4 → go to index 2
    At index 2: Include 3 → sum=0+3=3; Exclude 3 → sum=0
All leaf sums: [5, 2, 4, 3, 0]
Maximum: 5

Start at index 0:
Option 1: Include 1 → skip index 1 → go beyond array → sum=1
Option 2: Exclude 1 → go to index 1
  At index 1: Include 2 → sum=0+2=2; Exclude 2 → sum=0
All leaf sums: [1, 2, 0]
Maximum: 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Subset Generation with Constraints
ladders.desc: Generating all valid subsets of an array while skipping adjacent elements using recursion.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses the intermediate concept of recursively generating all valid subsets of an array while adhering to the constraint that no two adjacent elements can be included. The intuition is that we can build subsets incrementally by making decisions at each index: either include the current element (and then must skip the next), or exclude it. This is a constrained version of the classic subset generation problem. The recursion naturally handles the constraint through the parameter passing: when we include an element, we advance the index by 2 to skip the adjacent element; when we exclude, we advance by 1. The base case occurs when we reach beyond the array bounds, at which point we have a complete valid subset. This building block is essential because the maximum sum problem requires examining all such valid subsets. Time complexity is O(2^n) for generating all subsets, and space is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This subset generation implements the decision tree from L2 by actually producing the subsets. Common mistakes include generating invalid subsets with adjacent elements, or not properly terminating the recursion. Real code pattern: def generate_subsets(arr, i, current_subset, all_subsets): if i >= len(arr): all_subsets.append(current_subset.copy()); return; generate_subsets(arr, i+1, current_subset, all_subsets); current_subset.append(arr[i]); generate_subsets(arr, i+2, current_subset, all_subsets); current_subset.pop().

examples.trace:
Start: generate([1,2,3], 0, [], [])
At i=0:
  Exclude 1: call generate(arr, 1, [], all)
    At i=1:
      Exclude 2: call generate(arr, 2, [], all)
        At i=2:
          Exclude 3: call generate(arr, 3, [], all) → i>=3, add [] to all
          Include 3: add 3, call generate(arr, 4, [3], all) → add [3]
      Include 2: add 2, call generate(arr, 3, [2], all) → add [2]
        (Note: including 2 skips to i=3, so cannot include 3)
  Include 1: add 1, call generate(arr, 2, [1], all)
    At i=2:
      Exclude 3: call generate(arr, 3, [1], all) → add [1]
      Include 3: add 3, call generate(arr, 4, [1,3], all) → add [1,3]
All subsets collected: [], [3], [2], [2,3], [1], [1,3]

Start: generate([4,5], 0, [], [])
At i=0:
  Exclude 4: call generate(arr, 1, [], all)
    At i=1:
      Exclude 5: call generate(arr, 2, [], all) → add []
      Include 5: add 5, call generate(arr, 3, [5], all) → add [5]
  Include 4: add 4, call generate(arr, 2, [4], all) → add [4]
All subsets: [], [5], [4]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Calls with Index Advancement
ladders.desc: Making recursive calls while properly advancing array indices based on decisions.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of making recursive function calls with appropriate index advancement, which is crucial for the brute force approach. The intuition is that recursion allows us to break down the problem into smaller subproblems, and how we advance the index parameter determines whether we're respecting the non-adjacency constraint. When we decide to include an element at index i, we must skip the next element (i+1) to avoid adjacency, so we make a recursive call with index i+2. When we exclude the element at i, we can consider the very next element, so we call with i+1. This index advancement logic ensures that we never include two adjacent elements in our subset. The base case checks if the index has reached or exceeded the array length, returning 0 (or an empty subset in generation problems). This operation is basic but essential; getting the index arithmetic wrong leads to incorrect solutions. Time complexity for a single call chain is O(n), but overall it's O(2^n) due to multiple branches. Space is O(n) for the call stack. Connection to L3: This index advancement is used in the subset generation from L3 to create valid subsets. Common mistakes include using i+1 for inclusion (violating adjacency), or incorrect base case conditions. Real code pattern: The core operation is: recursive_call(arr, i+1) for exclusion and recursive_call(arr, i+2) for inclusion.

examples.trace:
Current index i=0. Decision: INCLUDE element at i=0.
Because we include arr[0], we cannot include arr[1] (adjacent).
Therefore, we skip index 1 entirely.
We make next recursive call starting from index i+2 = 0+2 = 2.
This ensures we never consider arr[1] in the same inclusion path.

Current index i=2. Decision: EXCLUDE element at i=2.
Since we're not taking arr[2], there's no restriction on arr[3].
We can consider arr[3] in the next step.
Therefore, we make next recursive call starting from index i+1 = 2+1 = 3.
This allows the possibility of including arr[3] in the next decision.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Basic recursion concept: function calling itself with a smaller problem and stopping at a base case.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept of recursion, essential for understanding the brute force approach. Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a smaller instance of the same problem. The intuition is divide-and-conquer: break a complex problem into smaller, similar subproblems until they become simple enough to solve directly. Every recursive function has two key parts: the recursive case (where the function calls itself with modified parameters) and the base case (a simple condition that stops the recursion). In the context of our problem, the recursive case involves making decisions about including or excluding elements, while the base case occurs when we've processed all elements (index beyond array bounds). Understanding recursion requires grasping the call stack: each recursive call adds a new frame to the stack, and returns unwind it. This is beginner-friendly because it mirrors mathematical induction and real-world hierarchical structures. Time complexity depends on how many recursive calls are made; here it's exponential. Space complexity is the maximum depth of the call stack. Connection to L4: Recursion provides the mechanism for the index advancement operations in L4. Common mistakes include missing base cases (leading to infinite recursion), or not reducing problem size in recursive calls. Real code pattern: def recursive_function(param): if base_case_condition: return base_value; else: smaller_param = modify(param); return combine(param, recursive_function(smaller_param)).

examples.trace:
factorial(3):
3 is not <=1, so recursive case: return 3 * factorial(2)
  factorial(2):
  2 is not <=1, so recursive case: return 2 * factorial(1)
    factorial(1):
    1 <=1, so base case: return 1
  Back to factorial(2): return 2 * 1 = 2
Back to factorial(3): return 3 * 2 = 6
Final result: 6

examples.input: Sum numbers from 1 to 3 using recursion
examples.trace:
sum_to_n(3):
3 is not <=0, so recursive case: return 3 + sum_to_n(2)
  sum_to_n(2):
  2 is not <=0, so recursive case: return 2 + sum_to_n(1)
    sum_to_n(1):
    1 is not <=0, so recursive case: return 1 + sum_to_n(0)
      sum_to_n(0):
      0 <=0, so base case: return 0
    Back to sum_to_n(1): return 1 + 0 = 1
  Back to sum_to_n(2): return 2 + 1 = 3
Back to sum_to_n(3): return 3 + 3 = 6
Final result: 6

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Use an array to store the maximum sum up to each index, choosing between including the current element (plus sum from two steps back) or excluding it (sum from previous step). Efficient for linear time and space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements Using DP Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with O(n) time and space to find maximum sum of non-adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: The maximum sum of non-adjacent elements problem requires selecting elements from an array such that no two selected elements are adjacent, maximizing their sum. The dynamic programming approach with an array works by building up solutions incrementally. At each index i, we have two choices: include the current element (nums[i]) plus the maximum sum from two steps back (dp[i-2]), or exclude it and take the maximum sum from the previous step (dp[i-1]). This works because the optimal solution up to index i must either include or exclude nums[i], and if we include it, we cannot include the adjacent element at i-1, so we must look at i-2. The recurrence relation is dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]) for i ≥ 2, with base cases dp[0] = nums[0] and dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]). The intuition is that we break the problem into overlapping subproblems—the maximum sum up to each index—and use previously computed results to avoid redundant calculations. This is a classic example of dynamic programming where optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems exist. The algorithm runs in O(n) time as it processes each element once, and O(n) space for the dp array. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of computing dp values at each index. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases (e.g., not handling arrays of size 1 or 2 properly), off-by-one errors in indices, and misunderstanding the recurrence (e.g., adding dp[i-1] instead of using max). Real code pattern: Initialize dp array of size n, set base cases, iterate from 2 to n-1 applying recurrence, return dp[n-1].

examples.trace: Input: nums = [3, 2, 7, 10], n = 4. Initialize dp = [0]*4. Base cases: dp[0] = nums[0] = 3. dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]) = max(3, 2) = 3. For i=2: dp[2] = max(dp[1], nums[2] + dp[0]) = max(3, 7 + 3) = max(3, 10) = 10. For i=3: dp[3] = max(dp[2], nums[3] + dp[1]) = max(10, 10 + 3) = max(10, 13) = 13. Return dp[3] = 13. Selected elements: 3 and 10 (non-adjacent, sum 13).

Input: nums = [5, 5, 10, 100, 10, 5], n = 6. Initialize dp = [0]*6. Base cases: dp[0] = 5. dp[1] = max(5, 5) = 5. For i=2: dp[2] = max(dp[1], nums[2] + dp[0]) = max(5, 10 + 5) = max(5, 15) = 15. For i=3: dp[3] = max(dp[2], nums[3] + dp[1]) = max(15, 100 + 5) = max(15, 105) = 105. For i=4: dp[4] = max(dp[3], nums[4] + dp[2]) = max(105, 10 + 15) = max(105, 25) = 105. For i=5: dp[5] = max(dp[4], nums[5] + dp[3]) = max(105, 5 + 105) = max(105, 110) = 110. Return dp[5] = 110. Selected elements: 5, 100, 5 (non-adjacent, sum 110).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing DP Value at Each Index
ladders.desc: Implement the recurrence relation to compute dp[i] from previous dp values and current element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the dynamic programming solution. For each index i (starting from 2), we compute dp[i] using the recurrence: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]). The intuition is that at position i, we have two options: either skip the current element (take dp[i-1], which represents the best sum up to i-1) or include the current element (add nums[i] to dp[i-2], since including nums[i] means we cannot include the adjacent element at i-1, so we must use the best sum up to i-2). This decision ensures we always maintain the non-adjacent constraint while maximizing the sum. The sub-problem is standalone because given base cases dp[0] and dp[1], and the array nums, we can compute any dp[i] independently. Time complexity for computing one dp[i] is O(1), and space is O(1) if we ignore input storage. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in a loop in the full solution to build the entire dp array. Common mistakes include using wrong indices (e.g., i-3 instead of i-2), not using max correctly, or modifying nums array inadvertently. Real code pattern: For a given i, return max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Non-Adjacent Sum
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence: max sum at i = max(previous sum, current + sum two steps back).
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines the dynamic programming solution: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]). This relation captures the essence of the problem—making optimal decisions at each step while respecting the non-adjacent constraint. The intuition is that to maximize the sum up to index i, we consider two scenarios: either we do not pick the current element (so we carry forward the best sum up to i-1, which is dp[i-1]), or we pick the current element (adding nums[i] to the best sum up to i-2, since picking i forbids picking i-1). The max operation ensures we choose the better of these two options. This works because the problem has optimal substructure: the optimal solution for the first i elements can be built from optimal solutions for smaller subproblems (i-1 and i-2). It also has overlapping subproblems, as the same dp values are reused. This logic is standalone as it can be applied given dp array and nums. Time and space are O(1) per computation. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the subroutine to compute dp[i]. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the recurrence (e.g., using dp[i-1] + nums[i] which violates non-adjacent rule), or not handling base cases separately. Real code pattern: Express the relation mathematically or in code as above.

examples.trace: Given: dp[i-1] = 3, dp[i-2] = 3, nums[i] = 7. Compute dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]) = max(3, 7 + 3) = max(3, 10) = 10.
Given: dp[i-1] = 15, dp[i-2] = 5, nums[i] = 100. Compute dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]) = max(15, 100 + 5) = max(15, 105) = 105.

ladders.title: Building Block — Handling Base Cases for DP Array
ladders.desc: Initialize the dp array for the first two indices based on array size.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up the initial values for the dynamic programming array, which are crucial for the recurrence to work correctly. For the non-adjacent sum problem, the base cases are: if the array has one element, the maximum sum is that element (dp[0] = nums[0]); if it has two elements, the maximum sum is the maximum of the two (dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1])). The intuition is that for a single element, we must take it (as there are no adjacency issues), and for two elements, we cannot take both (since they are adjacent), so we pick the larger one. These base cases provide the starting point for the recurrence relation to build upon. This is a standalone sub-problem because given an array nums, we can compute dp[0] and dp[1] independently. Time complexity is O(1), and space is O(1) for storing these values. Connection to L2: These base cases feed into the recurrence by providing dp[0] and dp[1] for computing dp[2] onwards. Common mistakes include not checking array length (leading to index errors), incorrectly setting dp[1] (e.g., always nums[1]), or missing the case for empty arrays (but problem assumes non-empty). Real code pattern: If n == 1, return nums[0]; if n >= 2, set dp[0] = nums[0], dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]).

examples.trace: Given: nums = [3,2,7,10], n = 4. Base cases: dp[0] = nums[0] = 3. dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]) = max(3, 2) = 3. So dp array starts as [3, 3].
Given: nums = [5,5,10,100,10,5], n = 6. Base cases: dp[0] = nums[0] = 5. dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]) = max(5, 5) = 5. So dp array starts as [5, 5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Adding Integers
ladders.desc: Perform max comparison and addition operations on integers.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental arithmetic tasks: adding two integers and finding the maximum of two integers. In the context of the non-adjacent sum problem, we need to compute expressions like nums[i] + dp[i-2] (addition) and then compare it with dp[i-1] using max (comparison). The intuition is simple: addition combines values, while max selects the larger value, which is essential for making optimal decisions in dynamic programming. This operation is standalone as it can be practiced with any integers. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in setting base cases (e.g., max(nums[0], nums[1])) and in the recurrence relation. Common mistakes include integer overflow (if numbers are large, but in typical problems, they fit within bounds), incorrect order of operations, or using min instead of max. Real code pattern: Use + for addition and max() function or conditional statements for comparison.

examples.trace: Given: a=3 (dp[i-2]), b=2 (dp[i-1]), c=7 (nums[i]). First, compute addition: c + a = 7 + 3 = 10. Then, compute max: max(b, 10) = max(2, 10) = 10.
Given: a=5 (dp[i-2]), b=15 (dp[i-1]), c=100 (nums[i]). First, compute addition: c + a = 100 + 5 = 105. Then, compute max: max(b, 105) = max(15, 105) = 105.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve elements from an array using indices, understanding zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves accessing elements in an array by their index. In programming, arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be accessed using an integer index. Typically, indexing starts at 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. For example, in an array nums = [3, 2, 7, 10], nums[0] is 3, nums[1] is 2, etc. The intuition is that indices provide a way to refer to specific positions in the array, which is essential for iterating over elements or performing operations at particular positions. This is a standalone concept as it can be practiced with any array. Time complexity for access is O(1), and space is O(1) per access. Connection to L4: Array access is needed to get nums[i], dp[i-1], and dp[i-2] in the basic operations. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in math but not in most programming languages), going out of bounds (e.g., accessing index equal to array length), or confusing index with value. Real code pattern: Use array_name[index] to retrieve the element.

examples.trace: Given: array nums = [3, 2, 7, 10]. Access element at index 2: nums[2] = 7. Indices: 0->3, 1->2, 2->7, 3->10.
Given: array nums = [5, 5, 10, 100, 10, 5]. Access element at index 3: nums[3] = 100. Indices: 0->5, 1->5, 2->10, 3->100, 4->10, 5->5.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming Space-Optimized O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array while maintaining only two variables for the previous two sums, updating them to compute the maximum without using extra space. Optimal for most cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements with Space-Optimized DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using space-optimized dynamic programming to find maximum sum of non-adjacent elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum sum we can obtain by selecting elements from an array such that no two selected elements are adjacent. The space-optimized dynamic programming approach solves this in O(n) time with O(1) space by maintaining only two variables instead of a full DP array. The intuition is that at each position i, the maximum sum up to i depends only on the maximum sums up to i-1 and i-2. We can think of it as making a decision at each element: either we take the current element plus the maximum sum up to i-2 (since we can't take i-1), or we skip the current element and take the maximum sum up to i-1. By iterating through the array, we update two variables: prev2 (maximum sum up to i-2) and prev1 (maximum sum up to i-1). At each step, we compute current = max(prev1, nums[i] + prev2), then shift prev2 = prev1 and prev1 = current. This way, we only need constant space. The algorithm starts with prev2 = 0 (no elements before index 0) and prev1 = nums[0] (only taking first element). For empty arrays, we return 0. For single-element arrays, we return that element. The time complexity is O(n) since we iterate through the array once. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only two variables. This approach is optimal for most cases because it avoids the overhead of maintaining a full DP array while preserving the same logic. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (especially for edge cases), forgetting to handle empty arrays, and incorrectly updating the variables (order matters). The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the key update logic repeatedly throughout the array.

examples.trace:
Initialize: prev2 = 0, prev1 = 2 (nums[0])
Index 1 (value 1): current = max(prev1=2, nums[1]+prev2=1+0=1) = 2, update prev2=2, prev1=2
Index 2 (value 4): current = max(prev1=2, nums[2]+prev2=4+2=6) = 6, update prev2=2, prev1=6
Index 3 (value 9): current = max(prev1=6, nums[3]+prev2=9+2=11) = 11, update prev2=6, prev1=11
Result: prev1 = 11 (select elements 2 and 9)

Initialize: prev2 = 0, prev1 = 3
Index 1 (2): current = max(3, 2+0=2) = 3, prev2=3, prev1=3
Index 2 (5): current = max(3, 5+3=8) = 8, prev2=3, prev1=8
Index 3 (10): current = max(8, 10+3=13) = 13, prev2=8, prev1=13
Index 4 (7): current = max(13, 7+8=15) = 15, prev2=13, prev1=15
Result: 15 (select elements 3, 5, and 7)

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — DP Update Step for Adjacent Constraint
ladders.desc: The core update logic that computes maximum sum at current position using previous two sums.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the fundamental update step in the space-optimized DP approach. Given three values: the current array element value, the maximum sum up to two positions back (prev2), and the maximum sum up to the previous position (prev1), it computes the maximum sum up to the current position. The intuition is based on the constraint that we cannot select adjacent elements. At the current position i, we have two choices: either we take the current element nums[i], in which case we must skip the previous element, so we add nums[i] to prev2 (the maximum sum up to i-2). Or we skip the current element, in which case we carry forward prev1 (the maximum sum up to i-1). The maximum of these two options gives the best sum up to i. Mathematically, current = max(prev1, nums[i] + prev2). This decision encapsulates the entire DP transition. The time complexity for this single step is O(1), and space is O(1) as it uses only a few variables. This sub-routine is repeated for each element in the array in L0. Common mistakes include confusing prev1 and prev2 roles, or incorrectly computing the max (e.g., forgetting to add nums[i] to prev2). The connection to L2 is that this update relies on the core insight of optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems. Real code pattern: def update_step(nums[i], prev2, prev1): return max(prev1, nums[i] + prev2).

examples.trace:
Option 1: skip current, take prev1 = 3
Option 2: take current + prev2 = 4 + 2 = 6
max(3, 6) = 6

Option 1: skip current, take prev1 = 8
Option 2: take current + prev2 = 7 + 5 = 12
max(8, 12) = 12

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure with Adjacency Constraint
ladders.desc: The principle that maximum sum up to i depends on solutions to smaller subproblems.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core dynamic programming insight for problems with adjacency constraints: the optimal solution up to position i can be constructed from optimal solutions of smaller subproblems. Specifically, for the non-adjacent elements problem, let dp[i] represent the maximum sum obtainable using elements up to index i. Then dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]). This recurrence relation shows optimal substructure because the best solution at i uses the best solutions at i-1 and i-2. The intuition is that when considering element i, we either exclude it (taking dp[i-1]) or include it (adding nums[i] to dp[i-2], since including i forces exclusion of i-1). This decomposition works because the problem exhibits overlapping subproblems—the same dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] are used multiple times. The base cases are dp[0] = nums[0] (only one element) and dp[-1] effectively 0 for empty prefix. This logic reduces an exponential brute-force problem to linear time. Time analysis for computing one dp[i] is O(1), but building the full table is O(n). Space for the full table is O(n), but we optimize to O(1) in L1. Common mistakes include incorrect recurrence (e.g., dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-1]) which violates adjacency), or mishandling base cases. Connection to L3: this logic relies on understanding how to break problems into subproblems. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*n; dp[0]=nums[0]; for i in range(1,n): dp[i]=max(dp[i-1], nums[i]+(dp[i-2] if i>=2 else 0)).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Array Traversal with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Processing an array sequentially while maintaining and updating state variables.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through an array from start to end, performing operations at each element while keeping track of one or more state variables that evolve based on the current element and previous state. In the context of our DP approach, the state variables are prev2 and prev1, representing the maximum sums up to two positions back and the previous position, respectively. The intuition is that many array problems can be solved by making a pass through the array and updating state incrementally, without needing to store the entire array's intermediate results. This technique is fundamental to space-optimized algorithms. The algorithm: initialize state variables appropriately for the problem (e.g., prev2=0, prev1=first element). Then for each subsequent element in the array, compute a new state based on the current element and previous states, and update the state variables for the next iteration. This requires careful ordering of updates to avoid overwriting values needed later. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements, with O(1) space for state variables. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (not handling empty arrays), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, and incorrect update order (e.g., updating prev1 before using it to compute current). Connection to L4: this building block uses basic operations like accessing array elements and updating variables. Real code pattern: prev2, prev1 = 0, nums[0]; for i in range(1, len(nums)): current = max(prev1, nums[i] + prev2); prev2, prev1 = prev1, current.

examples.trace:
Initialize: prev2=0, prev1=3
Index 1 (value 1): current = max(prev1=3, 1+prev2=1) = 3; update prev2=3, prev1=3
Index 2 (value 4): current = max(prev1=3, 4+prev2=4+3=7) = 7; update prev2=3, prev1=7
Final state: prev1=7

Initialize: prev2=0, prev1=2
Index 1 (value 5): current = max(2, 5+0=5) = 5; update prev2=2, prev1=5
Index 2 (value 3): current = max(5, 3+2=5) = 5; update prev2=5, prev1=5
Final state: prev1=5

testCases.input: Array [1], initial prev2=0, prev1=1, traverse (no iteration)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Maximum of Two Values with Addition
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of taking the maximum between two numbers, where one involves addition.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two values and selecting the larger one, where one of the values is computed by adding a current element to a previous sum. Specifically, given three numbers: a current value (from array), a previous sum (prev2), and another previous sum (prev1), we compute max(prev1, current + prev2). This is a combination of addition and comparison, which are elementary operations in programming. The intuition is simple: we evaluate two options and pick the better one. In the context of our problem, this operation decides whether to include or exclude the current element. The algorithm: first, compute the sum current + prev2. Then compare this sum with prev1 using a max function. The result is the maximum of the two. Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). This operation is performed at each step in the array traversal. Common mistakes include integer overflow (if numbers are very large, but typically handled by language), incorrect order of operations (e.g., max(prev1 + current, prev2)), or forgetting to add prev2. Connection to L5: this operation uses basic arithmetic and comparison concepts. Real code pattern: result = max(prev1, current + prev2).

examples.trace:
Compute current + prev2 = 3 + 4 = 7
Compare 7 and prev1=5
max(5, 7) = 7

Compute current + prev2 = 6 + 2 = 8
Compare 8 and prev1=9
max(9, 8) = 9

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Simple Comparisons
ladders.desc: Basic knowledge of arrays, accessing elements, and comparing numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers the prerequisite concepts needed to approach the problem: arrays and simple comparisons. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each accessible by an index starting from 0. For example, in array [2, 5, 3], the element at index 0 is 2, index 1 is 5, and index 2 is 3. Accessing an element is done via array[index]. Comparisons involve evaluating two numbers to determine which is larger, using operators like > or functions like max(). For instance, max(3, 5) returns 5. The intuition is that before solving complex problems, one must be comfortable with these basics: how to store data in an array, how to retrieve values, and how to make decisions based on numerical comparisons. In the context of our problem, we will need to traverse an array, access each element, and compare sums. The algorithm for this level is simply to practice accessing array elements and performing comparisons. Time complexity for accessing one element is O(1), and space depends on array size. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (e.g., accessing index 3 in a 3-element array, which has indices 0,1,2), confusing array values with indices, or incorrect comparison logic. Connection to L4: these operations are used in the max computation. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; value = arr[0]; result = max(value, 5).

examples.input: Array [4,7,1], access index 1 and compare with 5
examples.trace:
Access arr[1] = 7
Compare 7 and 5 using max: max(7,5) = 7

examples.input: Array [3,0,9], access index 2 and compare with index 0
examples.trace:
Access arr[2] = 9
Access arr[0] = 3
Compare 9 and 3: max(9,3) = 9

testCases.input: Array [1,3,5], access index 0, compare with index 1
Array [9,9,9], access index 1, compare with index 2
Array [4,5,6,7], access index 2, compare with index 3
Array [100,50], access index 0, compare with index 1
